request.question: Expressing a heterologous xylose isomerase from Piromyces sp. in Saccharomyces cerevisiae [CEN.PK](http://CEN.PK) will enable fermentation of xylose to ethanol at a yield of at least 0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 hours under anaerobic conditions, compared to zero ethanol production in the parental strain, due to the enzyme bypassing the native redox-imbalanced xylose reductase/xylitol dehydrogenase pathway.

hypothesis.intervention: Expressing a heterologous xylose isomerase from Piromyces sp. in Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK
hypothesis.threshold: at least 0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 hours
hypothesis.mechanistic_rationale: the enzyme bypassing the native redox-imbalanced xylose reductase/xylitol dehydrogenase pathway
hypothesis.readiness_rationale: The core fields are present, with a numeric ethanol yield threshold and mechanistic rationale. However, the ethanol/xylose quantification method is not specified, the comparator is only named as the parental strain without an exact strain identifier, and the expression approach lacks construct details, so scientist review is needed before CRO handoff.
clarifying_questions: What specific assay/analytical method will be used to quantify ethanol production and xylose consumption (e.g., HPLC)?
What exact parental strain identifier will be used as the comparator/control?
What is the specific expression construct or delivery method for the Piromyces sp. xylose isomerase (e.g., plasmid/integration system and promoter)?
hypothesis.literature_search_hint: Piromyces xylose isomerase Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK xylose ethanol fermentation
hypothesis.original_hypothesis: Expressing a heterologous xylose isomerase from Piromyces sp. in Saccharomyces cerevisiae [CEN.PK](http://CEN.PK) will enable fermentation of xylose to ethanol at a yield of at least 0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 hours under anaerobic conditions, compared to zero ethanol production in the parental strain, due to the enzyme bypassing the native redox-imbalanced xylose reductase/xylitol dehydrogenase pathway.

references.title: Xylose isomerase overexpression along with engineering of the pentose phosphate pathway and evolutionary engineering enable rapid xylose utilization and ethanol production by Saccharomyces cerevisiae.
references.relevance_note: This paper directly concerns xylose isomerase overexpression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae for xylose utilization and ethanol production, matching the core metabolic-engineering fermentation experiment; the title suggests pathway engineering/evolutionary engineering may accompany XI expression, but it still covers the same intervention class and outcome context.

references.title: Development of Efficient Xylose Fermentation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae: Xylose Isomerase as a Key Component
references.relevance_note: This paper explicitly frames efficient xylose fermentation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae with xylose isomerase as a key component, which is the same experimental context of engineering S. cerevisiae to ferment xylose to ethanol via XI expression.

references.title: Metal Dependence of the Xylose Isomerase from Piromyces sp. E2 Explored by Activity Profiling and Protein Crystallography
references.relevance_note: This paper specifically identifies xylose isomerase from Piromyces sp. E2 as enabling Saccharomyces cerevisiae to ferment xylose to ethanol, directly matching the heterologous Piromyces XI intervention and fermentation outcome, although it focuses on biochemical/structural characterization rather than the original strain-construction fermentation test.

literature_qc.explanation: On the intervention dimension, the retrieved set includes xylose isomerase expression/overexpression in S. cerevisiae [0,3] and specifically Piromyces sp. E2 xylose isomerase used to equip S. cerevisiae for xylose-to-ethanol fermentation [5]. On the biological-system dimension, multiple papers are in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, and the CEN.PK background is strongly implicated by the classic xylose-isomerase yeast engineering literature even though not all abstracts/titles state the exact strain. On the experiment-type dimension, the papers concern metabolic engineering and fermentation of xylose to ethanol, so the core experiment has already been described even if the exact comparator or fermentation thresholds are not specified here.
literature_qc.recommended_action: A paper matching this experiment was found in the scientific literature. Review it before proceeding. If this is intentional replication, state that explicitly. If not, differentiate your approach.

protocol_candidates.protocol_name: Standard metabolic_engineering_fermentation protocol for expressing a heterologous xylose isomerase from piromyces sp. in saccharomyces cerevisiae cen.pk
protocol_candidates.adaptation_notes: Stub mode — no live protocols.io search performed. This is a placeholder candidate.
limitations: Stub output only — real protocols.io search requires PROTOCOLS_IO_TOKEN.

evidence_claims.claim: Post-thaw viability directly tests the stated biological outcome.
evidence_claims.support_summary: Outcome is explicitly present in user hypothesis.

evidence_claims.claim: Trehalose membrane stabilization rationale supports intervention plausibility.
evidence_claims.support_summary: Mechanistic rationale is explicitly stated in user input.

evidence_claims.claim: Expected +15 percentage-point threshold may be optimistic without pilot calibration.
evidence_claims.support_summary: Effect size is user-defined but not validated in this run.

risks.description: Standard experimental risk — the approach has literature support but execution details should be reviewed before proceeding.
risks.mitigation: Confirm all materials, controls, and readiness criteria with a scientist before execution.

plan.objective: Determine whether heterologous expression of Piromyces sp. xylose isomerase enables Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK to convert xylose to ethanol at ≥0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 h under oxygen-limited fermentation.
plan.experimental_design: A pilot metabolic-engineering fermentation compares CEN.PK transformed with a URA3-marked plasmid expressing Piromyces sp. xylose isomerase against parental CEN.PK and an uninoculated medium blank in biological triplicate. Transformants are selected, verified by colony PCR, precultured under selective conditions, transferred to defined xylose fermentation medium at matched biomass, and sampled over 48 h for HPLC quantification of xylose, ethanol, xylitol, glycerol, and acetate. The proceed gate is ethanol yield from the engineered strain relative to xylose consumed, with parental strain defining background ethanol formation on xylose.
controls: Previously validated Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK-XI reference strain harboring Piromyces sp. E2 xylA on a URA3 plasmid with documented ethanol yield ≥0.35 g/g xylose in 48 h.
Uninoculated 50 mL fermentation containing 20 g/L D-xylose defined medium in 120 mL serum bottles under N2 at 30 °C for 48 h.

step_by_step_protocol.description:
DAY 0 (PREP) | 15 min | Setup and scheduling
Reserve a New Brunswick Innova 44R shaking incubator at 30 °C, an Agilent 1260 Infinity II HPLC system at 55 °C, a Bio-Rad T100 thermal cycler at 95 °C denaturation capability, and a Thermo Scientific Heracell VIOS incubator at 30 °C for Days 1–7.
Expected outcome: Instrument reservations cover at least 7 consecutive days with no scheduling conflicts longer than 30 min.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 1.5 h | Reagent preparation
Autoclave 1.0 L of 2× YP base containing 20 g/L yeast extract and 40 g/L peptone at 121 °C for 20 min in a Systec VX-95 autoclave.
Expected outcome: Sterilization cycle reaches 121 °C for 20 min with autoclave printout confirming F0 ≥15.
Safety: Hot-liquid burn risk — wear heat-resistant gloves and face protection.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 30 min | Reagent preparation
Filter-sterilize 500 mL of 400 g/L D-xylose stock through a 0.22 µm PES bottle-top filter at 22 °C into a sterile 500 mL bottle.
Expected outcome: Clear sterile xylose stock with no visible particulates and final volume ≥480 mL.

DAY 0 (PREP) | 45 min | Reagent preparation
Dispense 25 mL aliquots of SD-URA agar containing 6.7 g/L yeast nitrogen base without amino acids, 1.92 g/L yeast synthetic drop-out medium lacking uracil, 20 g/L glucose, and 20 g/L agar into 90 mm sterile Petri dishes at 50 °C.
Expected outcome: At least 20 plates contain 25 ± 2 mL agar with smooth surfaces and no bubbles covering >5% of plate area.
Safety: Molten agar burn risk — wear thermal gloves and lab coat.

DAY 1 | 10 min | Yeast transformation
Thaw one 100 µL aliquot of chemically competent Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK cells on ice at 0–4 °C for 10 min.
Expected outcome: Cell suspension is fully thawed with no ice crystals visible in the 100 µL aliquot.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — decontaminate yeast waste with 10% bleach for 30 min.

DAY 1 | 10 min | Yeast transformation
Mix 50 µL competent CEN.PK cells with 1.0 µg pURA3-TDH3-Piromyces-xylA plasmid DNA, 5 µL denatured salmon sperm carrier DNA at 10 mg/mL, 240 µL 50% w/v PEG 3350, 36 µL 1.0 M lithium acetate, and 59 µL sterile water in a 1.5 mL tube at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Transformation mixture volume is 390 ± 10 µL with visibly homogeneous viscosity after mixing.
Safety: Lithium acetate is irritant — wear nitrile gloves and eye protection.

DAY 1 | 30 min | Yeast transformation
Incubate the transformation mixture at 30 °C for 30 min in an Eppendorf ThermoMixer C at 300 rpm.
Expected outcome: Tube contents remain suspended with no pellet larger than 2 mm at the tube bottom.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — keep tube closed outside the biosafety cabinet.

DAY 1 | 20 min | Yeast transformation
Heat-shock the transformation mixture at 42 °C for 20 min in an Eppendorf ThermoMixer C without shaking.
Expected outcome: Tube reaches 42 ± 1 °C for the full 20 min interval according to block display.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — keep tube closed outside the biosafety cabinet.

DAY 1 | 5 min | Yeast transformation
Centrifuge the transformation mixture at 3,000 × g for 2 min at 22 °C in an Eppendorf 5424R centrifuge.
Expected outcome: A compact yeast pellet of 1–3 mm diameter is visible at the bottom of the tube.
Safety: Balance tubes within 0.01 g before centrifugation.

DAY 1 | 5 min | Yeast transformation
Resuspend the yeast pellet in 200 µL sterile 0.9% w/v NaCl at 22 °C by pipetting 10 strokes with a P200 tip.
Expected outcome: Suspension is uniform with no clumps larger than 1 mm visible.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — work in a disinfected biosafety cabinet.

DAY 1 | 10 min | Yeast transformation
Spread 100 µL resuspended yeast cells onto each SD-URA agar plate with sterile 3 mm glass beads at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Cell suspension is distributed over ≥90% of the agar surface on each plate.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — collect used beads into 10% bleach for 30 min.

DAY 1 | 48 h | Yeast transformation
Incubate SD-URA transformation plates at 30 °C for 48 h in a Thermo Scientific Heracell VIOS incubator.
Expected outcome: Transformation plates show 20–300 uracil-prototrophic colonies per 100 µL plating volume by 48 h.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — keep plates sealed with one layer of Parafilm.

DAY 3 | 15 min | Transformant verification
Pick 8 single colonies into 20 µL sterile 20 mM NaOH in 0.2 mL PCR tubes at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Each PCR tube contains one visible yeast cell suspension with turbidity above sterile NaOH blank.
Safety: NaOH is caustic — wear nitrile gloves and eye protection.

DAY 3 | 15 min | Transformant verification
Lyse colony suspensions at 95 °C for 10 min in a Bio-Rad T100 thermal cycler.
Expected outcome: Lysates show no intact colony clumps larger than 0.5 mm after heating.
Safety: Hot-tube burn risk — allow tubes to cool to ≤30 °C before opening.

DAY 3 | 1.5 h | Transformant verification
Amplify xylA insert from 1 µL colony lysate in a 25 µL PCR containing 1× Q5 Hot Start Master Mix, 0.5 µM forward primer, and 0.5 µM reverse primer for 35 cycles of 98 °C for 10 s, 60 °C for 20 s, and 72 °C for 90 s.
Expected outcome: PCR reaction completes 35 cycles with expected amplicon size approximately 1.3 kb for positive colonies.
Safety: Use aerosol-resistant tips to prevent amplicon contamination.

DAY 3 | 50 min | Transformant verification
Electrophorese 5 µL PCR product on a 1.0% w/v agarose gel in 1× TAE at 100 V for 35 min using a Bio-Rad Mini-Sub Cell GT.
Expected outcome: At least 3 of 8 colonies show a single xylA band at 1.2–1.4 kb and no-template control shows no visible band.
Safety: Wear UV/blue-light eye protection when imaging stained gels.

DAY 3 | 20 min | Seed culture
Inoculate three PCR-positive xylA colonies, one CEN.PK-XI positive-control colony, and three parental CEN.PK colonies into separate 50 mL baffled flasks containing 10 mL YPD with 20 g/L glucose at 30 °C.
Expected outcome: Seven flasks each contain 10 mL broth with one defined yeast inoculum and initial OD600 <0.05.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — label engineered cultures with GMO identifier.

DAY 3 | 16 h | Seed culture
Shake seed cultures at 30 °C and 200 rpm for 16 h in a New Brunswick Innova 44R incubator with a 25 mm orbit.
Expected outcome: Seed cultures reach OD600 3.0–8.0 after 16 h.
Safety: BSL-1 GMO handling — keep flask caps secured in the shaker.

DAY 4 | 20 min | Fermentation inoculum preparation
Measure OD600 of each seed culture at 600 nm using a Thermo Scientific NanoDrop OneC after diluting 100 µL culture into 900 µL sterile 0.9% w/v NaCl.
Expected outcome: Diluted OD600 readings fall within 0.2–0.8 absorbance units for back-calculation.
Safety: Disinfect pedestal with 70% ethanol after yeast measurements.

DAY 4 | 30 min | Fermentation setup
Dispense 50 mL fermentation medium containing 20 g/L D-xylose, 6.7 g/L yeast nitrogen base without amino acids, 1.92 g/L complete supplement mixture, 10 mg/L ergosterol, 420 mg/L Tween 80, and 50 mM potassium phthalate pH 5.0 into each 120 mL serum bottle at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Each serum bottle contains 50.0 ± 0.5 mL medium with pH 5.0 ± 0.1.

DAY 4 | 1.5 h | Fermentation setup
Sparge each serum bottle with sterile N2 gas at 100 mL/min for 10 min through a 0.22 µm PTFE inline filter at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Dissolved oxygen probe reading in a representative bottle is ≤0.5 mg/L at the end of sparging.
Safety: Nitrogen asphyxiation risk — sparge in a ventilated area with oxygen monitor active.

DAY 4 | 20 min | Fermentation setup
Seal each serum bottle with a 20 mm butyl rubber stopper and an aluminum crimp cap at 22 °C using a Wheaton hand crimper.
Expected outcome: Each bottle holds pressure for 1 min without audible gas leakage when gently squeezed at the stopper.
Safety: Crimping pinch hazard — keep fingers clear of crimper jaws.

DAY 4 | 30 min | Fermentation inoculation
Inoculate sealed fermentation bottles through the stopper with calculated seed-culture volumes to achieve OD600 1.0 in 50 mL final volume for engineered, parental, and positive-control cultures.
Expected outcome: Each inoculated bottle has calculated starting OD600 1.00 ± 0.05 with final volume 50.0 ± 0.5 mL.
Safety: Needlestick risk — use a sharps container immediately after syringe use.

DAY 4 | 48 h | Fermentation
Incubate fermentation bottles at 30 °C and 120 rpm for 48 h in a New Brunswick Innova 44R shaker with a 25 mm orbit.
Expected outcome: Bottle pressure remains below visible stopper deformation and cultures remain suspended with no settled biomass layer thicker than 2 mm.
Safety: Pressurized-bottle risk — inspect stopper integrity before handling.

DAY 6 | 25 min | Fermentation sampling
Withdraw 1.0 mL culture broth from each serum bottle at 48 h using a sterile 1 mL syringe and 23G needle at 22 °C.
Expected outcome: Each bottle yields 1.0 ± 0.1 mL broth sample without visible stopper coring particles.
Safety: Needlestick risk — use a sharps container immediately after syringe use.

DAY 6 | 15 min | Sample clarification
Centrifuge 1.0 mL fermentation broth samples at 16,000 × g for 10 min at 4 °C in an Eppendorf 5424R centrifuge.
Expected outcome: Supernatants are visibly clear with a compact yeast pellet at the tube bottom.
Safety: Balance tubes within 0.01 g before centrifugation.

DAY 6 | 20 min | Sample clarification
Filter 700 µL clarified supernatant through a 0.22 µm PVDF centrifugal filter at 12,000 × g for 5 min at 4 °C.
Expected outcome: Each HPLC vial contains ≥500 µL particle-free filtrate.
Safety: Treat filtrates as BSL-1 GMO-derived material until HPLC vials are sealed.

DAY 6 | 2 h | HPLC calibration
Inject 10 µL mixed calibration standards containing 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, and 20.0 g/L D-xylose and ethanol onto an Aminex HPX-87H column at 55 °C with 5 mM H2SO4 mobile phase flowing at 0.6 mL/min.
Expected outcome: Calibration curves for xylose and ethanol have R² ≥0.995 and back-calculated standard errors ≤10%.
Safety: 5 mM H2SO4 is corrosive — wear gloves, lab coat, and eye protection.

DAY 6 | 6 h | HPLC quantification
Inject 10 µL filtered fermentation supernatant onto an Aminex HPX-87H column at 55 °C with 5 mM H2SO4 mobile phase flowing at 0.6 mL/min for 35 min per sample.
Expected outcome: Chromatograms contain resolved xylose and ethanol peaks with retention-time drift ≤0.2 min and signal-to-noise ratio ≥10 for ethanol.
Safety: 5 mM H2SO4 is corrosive — use secondary containment for solvent reservoirs.

DAY 6 | 2 h | Data analysis and interpretation
Calculate ethanol yield as grams ethanol produced per gram xylose consumed at 48 h and compare engineered, parental, positive-control, and uninoculated groups using one-way ANOVA with Tukey HSD post hoc testing at α=0.05 plus Shapiro-Wilk normality testing for n=3 biological replicates.
Expected outcome: Engineering success is concluded only if engineered CEN.PK reaches ≥0.4 g ethanol/g xylose consumed by 48 h with Tukey-adjusted p<0.05 versus parental CEN.PK.

assumptions: The Piromyces sp. xylose isomerase expression plasmid is sequence-verified, yeast-compatible, and uses a constitutive promoter such as TDH3 or TEF1 with a URA3 selection marker.
Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK parental and engineered derivatives are handled under BSL-1 containment and do not require special permits beyond institutional GMO approval.
Defined fermentation medium containing 20 g/L D-xylose provides sufficient nitrogen, vitamins, minerals, and anaerobic growth factors for oxygen-limited yeast fermentation.
HPLC calibration over 0.1–20 g/L for xylose and ethanol is linear enough to quantify yield differences across 48 h.
decision_criteria: Proceed to strain-optimization scale-up if engineered CEN.PK produces ≥0.4 g ethanol/g xylose consumed at 48 h with xylose consumption ≥5.0 g/L and one-way ANOVA/Tukey-adjusted p<0.05 versus parental strain.
Repeat as a larger pilot if engineered CEN.PK produces 0.25–0.39 g ethanol/g xylose consumed or if biological-triplicate coefficient of variation for ethanol yield is >20%.
Stop the construct workflow if colony PCR fails for ≥8 colonies, if no transformants grow after 72 h at 30 °C, or if engineered ethanol yield is ≤0.10 g/g xylose at 48 h.
reproducibility_notes: Use the same CEN.PK parental lot, record passage number, and limit yeast propagation to ≤5 passages before fermentation.
Use one sequence-verified plasmid lot for all engineered biological replicates and record promoter, terminator, marker, and xylA accession sequence.
Use a single batch of D-xylose defined medium and document pH meter calibration with pH 4.01 and pH 7.00 standards on the day of medium preparation.
Calibrate the Agilent 1260 Infinity II HPLC refractive index detector with fresh xylose, ethanol, xylitol, glycerol, and acetate standards on the sample-analysis day.
Standardize serum-bottle headspace volume, nitrogen sparging time, closure type, shaking orbit, and inoculation OD600 across all fermentation bottles.
feedback_incorporated: Reagent Preparation › materials (rating 5/5): something is off

materials.item_name: New Brunswick Innova 44R shaking incubator reservation/use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Internal recharge rates vary widely; estimate assumes academic shared-equipment daily access.

materials.item_name: Agilent 1260 Infinity II HPLC system with RID/appropriate detector use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: HPLC charges depend on detector, column ownership, and operator support.

materials.item_name: Bio-Rad T100 thermal cycler use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Many departments do not recharge PCR cycler use; daily access fee is a planning estimate.

materials.item_name: Thermo Scientific Heracell VIOS incubator use fee
materials.quantity: 2 days active plate incubation within 7-day reservation
materials.uncertainty_note: Estimate assumes shared incubator space rather than full incubator rental.

materials.item_name: Systec VX-95 autoclave use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Autoclave recharge may be bundled into facility overhead at some institutions.

materials.item_name: Analytical balance/top-loading balance
materials.uncertainty_note: Needed for weighing media components; usually no direct recharge.

materials.item_name: Hot plate or microwave for melting agar
materials.uncertainty_note: Estimate represents shared-equipment use and may be zero if in-lab.

materials.item_name: Yeast extract
materials.quantity: 20 g for 1 L 2× YP base plus small contingency
materials.uncertainty_note: Commodity media component; price varies with package size.

materials.item_name: Peptone, bacteriological/yeast peptone
materials.uncertainty_note: Commodity media component; estimate assumes bulk academic pricing.

materials.item_name: D-xylose
materials.quantity: 200 g for 500 mL of 400 g/L stock plus fermentation/calibration use
materials.uncertainty_note: D-xylose pricing depends strongly on grade and package size.

materials.item_name: 0.22 µm PES bottle-top filter, 500 mL capacity
materials.uncertainty_note: Bottle-top filter price varies by membrane and sterility packaging.

materials.item_name: Sterile 500 mL media/storage bottle
materials.uncertainty_note: Reusable glass bottles are cheaper per use; sterile disposable bottles cost more.

materials.item_name: Yeast nitrogen base without amino acids
materials.quantity: ~12 g for 500 mL SD-URA agar and 600 mL fermentation medium
materials.uncertainty_note: YNB price depends on formulation and package size.

materials.item_name: Yeast synthetic drop-out medium lacking uracil
materials.uncertainty_note: Drop-out supplement pricing varies by supplier and amino-acid formulation.

materials.item_name: D-glucose
materials.quantity: 10 g for SD-URA agar plus 1.4 g for YPD seed cultures
materials.uncertainty_note: Commodity reagent; cost negligible at this scale.

materials.item_name: Agar, microbiological grade
materials.uncertainty_note: Agar cost varies modestly with grade.

materials.item_name: Sterile 90 mm Petri dishes
materials.uncertainty_note: Bulk sleeve pricing assumed.

materials.item_name: Heat-resistant gloves and face protection allocation
materials.uncertainty_note: PPE is reusable; cost is prorated for this experiment.

materials.item_name: Class II biosafety cabinet use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Needed for aseptic/GMO handling; facility recharge may be absent or bundled.

materials.item_name: Chemically competent Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK cells
materials.uncertainty_note: CEN.PK strain costs depend on source and whether already available in-house.

materials.item_name: pURA3-TDH3-Piromyces-xylA plasmid DNA
materials.quantity: 1 µg working DNA; assumes plasmid already prepared or purchased as construct aliquot
materials.uncertainty_note: Custom plasmid synthesis/cloning could cost substantially more if not already available.

materials.item_name: Salmon sperm carrier DNA, denatured, 10 mg/mL
materials.uncertainty_note: Cost is prorated from a stock vial; purchase price is much higher than single-use cost.

materials.item_name: Polyethylene glycol 3350 (PEG 3350), sterile 50% w/v solution
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated reagent cost; prepared stock requires sterile water and filtration/autoclaving.

materials.item_name: Lithium acetate, 1.0 M sterile solution
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated cost is very small; purchase bottle cost is higher.

materials.item_name: Sterile molecular biology water
materials.quantity: ~10 mL across transformation and PCR controls
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from a nuclease-free water bottle.

materials.item_name: Eppendorf ThermoMixer C use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Daily shared-equipment fee estimate.

materials.item_name: Eppendorf 5424R refrigerated microcentrifuge use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Some facilities do not charge separately for benchtop centrifuges.

materials.item_name: Sterile 0.9% w/v sodium chloride solution
materials.quantity: ~100 mL for resuspension and OD dilutions
materials.uncertainty_note: Can be prepared in-house at very low cost; sterile commercial saline is more expensive.

materials.item_name: Sterile 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes
materials.uncertainty_note: Includes balance and spare tubes.

materials.item_name: Sterile 3 mm glass beads for spreading
materials.uncertainty_note: Reusable if cleaned, but treated here as single-experiment consumable due to GMO contact.

materials.item_name: Parafilm for sealing plates
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from a roll.

materials.item_name: Household bleach/sodium hypochlorite disinfectant for 10% bleach waste
materials.uncertainty_note: Commodity disinfectant cost is minimal.

materials.item_name: Sterile toothpicks or inoculation loops for colony picking
materials.uncertainty_note: Includes extra picks for colony selection.

materials.item_name: Sodium hydroxide, 20 mM sterile solution
materials.quantity: ~1 mL for 8 colony lysates plus blank
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from laboratory stock; handling hazard more important than cost.

materials.item_name: 0.2 mL PCR tubes or strip tubes
materials.uncertainty_note: Includes colony lysis and PCR reactions.

materials.item_name: Q5 Hot Start High-Fidelity 2× Master Mix
materials.uncertainty_note: Cost is prorated from a 100–500 reaction master mix pack.

materials.item_name: xylA forward primer
materials.uncertainty_note: Primer synthesis cost varies with length and purification.

materials.item_name: xylA reverse primer
materials.uncertainty_note: Primer synthesis cost varies with length and purification.

materials.item_name: No-template PCR control reaction
materials.uncertainty_note: Control cost includes master mix/primers allocated to one reaction.

materials.item_name: Bio-Rad Mini-Sub Cell GT gel electrophoresis tank and power supply use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Shared gel systems are often not recharged; estimate supports cost accounting.

materials.item_name: Gel documentation system or blue-light transilluminator use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Fee varies depending on imager and facility policy.

materials.item_name: Agarose for 1.0% PCR gel
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from bulk agarose.

materials.item_name: 50× TAE buffer or prepared 1× TAE
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from concentrated stock.

materials.item_name: DNA gel stain, non-mutagenic (e.g., SYBR Safe/GelRed)
materials.uncertainty_note: Stain cost varies significantly by product and staining format.

materials.item_name: 6× DNA loading dye
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated from lab stock.

materials.item_name: 1 kb DNA ladder
materials.uncertainty_note: Cost depends on ladder brand and loading amount.

materials.item_name: YPD broth components or prepared YPD medium
materials.quantity: ~100 mL for seven 10 mL seed cultures plus excess
materials.uncertainty_note: Uses yeast extract, peptone, glucose; cost assumes in-house preparation.

materials.item_name: Sterile 50 mL baffled culture flasks with vented closures
materials.uncertainty_note: Glass reusable flasks would reduce single-experiment consumable cost.

materials.item_name: CEN.PK-XI positive-control colony/strain
materials.quantity: 1 control strain/colony used to seed triplicate controls
materials.uncertainty_note: Cost depends on whether the strain is obtained from a repository, collaborator, or already held in-house.

materials.item_name: Parental Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK colonies/strain for negative control
materials.uncertainty_note: Repository strain acquisition may be unnecessary if parental CEN.PK is already in the lab.

materials.item_name: Thermo Scientific NanoDrop OneC use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: NanoDrop OD600 use is typically not separately charged in many labs.

materials.item_name: Disposable cuvettes or microvolume pedestal wipes/lint-free tissues
materials.uncertainty_note: NanoDrop measurements mainly consume wipes and small tubes rather than cuvettes.

materials.item_name: 70% ethanol disinfectant
materials.uncertainty_note: Commodity disinfectant; cost is prorated.

materials.item_name: 120 mL serum bottles
materials.uncertainty_note: Reusable serum bottles may be autoclaved and reused, lowering per-experiment cost.

materials.item_name: Yeast nitrogen base without amino acids for fermentation medium
materials.uncertainty_note: Listed separately from plate medium to attribute fermentation use.

materials.item_name: Complete supplement mixture (CSM)
materials.uncertainty_note: CSM formulations and pricing vary by supplier.

materials.item_name: Ergosterol
materials.uncertainty_note: Often prepared as ethanolic stock; cost prorated from a small vial.

materials.item_name: Tween 80 (polysorbate 80)
materials.uncertainty_note: Prorated cost is negligible.

materials.item_name: Potassium hydrogen phthalate/phthalate buffer components, pH 5.0
materials.quantity: ~6 g equivalent for 600 mL of 50 mM buffer
materials.uncertainty_note: Small cost; may require pH adjustment with acid/base from stock.

materials.item_name: pH meter use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: pH meter costs usually represent maintenance/electrode allocation rather than direct rental.

materials.item_name: Nitrogen gas, high purity cylinder gas
materials.uncertainty_note: Cost excludes cylinder rental and assumes central gas or cylinder already available.

materials.item_name: 0.22 µm PTFE inline sterile gas filters
materials.quantity: 12 filters or one validated reusable setup
materials.uncertainty_note: If one filter is used sequentially aseptically, cost may be much lower; single-use per bottle is conservative.

materials.item_name: Gas regulator/flow meter and sparging manifold use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Shared gas hardware may not have a recharge fee.

materials.item_name: Dissolved oxygen probe/meter use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: DO probe availability and calibration consumables can change cost.

materials.item_name: Oxygen monitor for nitrogen sparging safety
materials.uncertainty_note: Safety monitor may be building-provided; cost is prorated.

materials.item_name: 20 mm butyl rubber stoppers
materials.uncertainty_note: Stoppers are often single-use after piercing.

materials.item_name: 20 mm aluminum crimp caps
materials.uncertainty_note: Bulk pricing assumed.

materials.item_name: Wheaton hand crimper use fee
materials.uncertainty_note: Usually owned by the lab; estimate is equipment allocation.

materials.item_name: Sterile syringes for inoculation through stoppers
materials.uncertainty_note: Assumes one sterile syringe per bottle.

materials.item_name: Sterile needles for inoculation through stoppers

line_items.item_name: New Brunswick Innova 44R shaking incubator reservation/use fee
line_items.uncertainty_note: Internal recharge rates vary widely; estimate assumes academic shared-equipment daily access.

line_items.item_name: Agilent 1260 Infinity II HPLC system with RID/appropriate detector use fee
line_items.uncertainty_note: HPLC charges depend on detector, column ownership, and operator support.

line_items.item_name: Bio-Rad T100 thermal cycler use fee
line_items.uncertainty_note: Many departments do not recharge PCR cycler use; daily access fee is a planning estimate.

line_items.item_name: Thermo Scientific Heracell VIOS incubator use fee
line_items.quantity: 2 days active plate incubation within 7-day reservation
line_items.uncertainty_note: Estimate assumes shared incubator space rather than full incubator rental.

line_items.item_name: Systec VX-95 autoclave use fee
line_items.uncertainty_note: Autoclave recharge may be bundled into facility overhead at some institutions.

line_items.item_name: Analytical balance/top-loading balance
line_items.uncertainty_note: Needed for weighing media components; usually no direct recharge.

line_items.item_name: Peptone, bacteriological/yeast peptone
line_items.uncertainty_note: Commodity media component; estimate assumes bulk academic pricing.

line_items.item_name: D-xylose

line_items.item_name: 0.22 µm PES bottle-top filter, 500 mL capacity
line_items.uncertainty_note: Bottle-top filter price varies by membrane and sterility packaging.

line_items.item_name: Sterile 500 mL media/storage bottle

line_items.item_name: Sterile 90 mm Petri dishes
line_items.uncertainty_note: Bulk sleeve pricing assumed.

uncertainty_notes: Several instruments are listed both as reservation/use allocations and as sub-protocol active-use fees; for an internal budget, choose either reservation-based or run-time-based charging to avoid double-counting.

Custom biological materials drive the largest uncertainty: the pURA3-TDH3-Piromyces-xylA plasmid and CEN.PK control strains may be already available, obtained from collaborators, or require paid synthesis/repository purchase.
Prices are typical 2024–2025 academic European estimates; institutional framework discounts, VAT treatment, and facility recharge models can shift totals substantially.

phases.phase_name: Day 0: Instrument booking and media/reagent preparation
bottlenecks: Consecutive 7-day shaker/incubator and HPLC availability
Autoclave cycle queue and cool-down time for liquid media
Filter clogging during high-concentration xylose stock sterilization
SD-URA plate drying/solidification quality before transformation

phases.phase_name: Day 1: Yeast transformation setup, heat shock, recovery plating
dependencies: Day 0: Instrument booking and media/reagent preparation
bottlenecks: Competent CEN.PK cell viability after thaw
Plasmid DNA quality and concentration
ThermoMixer block availability for 30 °C incubation and 42 °C heat shock
Uneven spreading or overly wet SD-URA plates reducing colony isolation

phases.phase_name: Days 1-3: Selective incubation of transformation plates
dependencies: Day 1: Yeast transformation setup, heat shock, recovery plating
bottlenecks: Slow colony emergence requiring an extra 24 h incubation
Low transformation efficiency yielding too few colonies
Incubator temperature deviations at 30 °C
Contamination on selective plates

phases.phase_name: Day 3: Colony PCR verification and agarose gel electrophoresis
dependencies: Days 1-3: Selective incubation of transformation plates
bottlenecks: PCR false negatives from incomplete yeast colony lysis
Thermal cycler availability for 35-cycle Q5 PCR
Gel imaging system availability and band resolution around 1.3 kb
Insufficient number of PCR-positive colonies requiring repeat transformation or additional colony screening

phases.phase_name: Day 3: Inoculate seed cultures from verified colonies
dependencies: Day 3: Colony PCR verification and agarose gel electrophoresis
bottlenecks: Need at least three confirmed xylA transformants before inoculation
Accurate colony picking to avoid mixed cultures
Availability of baffled flasks and 30 °C shaking incubator space

dependencies: Day 3: Inoculate seed cultures from verified colonies
bottlenecks: Seed cultures may undergrow or overgrow outside OD600 3-8 depending on strain fitness
Shaker capacity and 200 rpm operation overnight
Contamination or mixed inocula discovered next day
Differences in growth between engineered, parental, and positive-control strains

phases.phase_name: Day 4: OD measurement and anaerobic fermentation setup/inoculation
bottlenecks: OD600 dilution accuracy for inoculating all bottles to OD600 1.0
Serum bottle, stopper, and crimp-cap availability
Nitrogen sparging manifold or gas cylinder availability
Achieving low dissolved oxygen without compromising sterility
Needle handling and stopper leakage during inoculation

dependencies: Day 4: OD measurement and anaerobic fermentation setup/inoculation
bottlenecks: Shaker availability for sealed serum bottles at 30 °C and 120 rpm for full 48 h
Biological variability in xylose utilization by transformants
Stopper leakage or pressure buildup in sealed bottles
Unexpected slow fermentation requiring additional time-point sampling

phases.phase_name: Day 6: Fermentation sampling and HPLC sample clarification
bottlenecks: Needle coring or sample loss during serum bottle sampling
Centrifuge availability at 4 °C
Filter membrane clogging from residual yeast biomass
Maintaining sample identity across engineered, parental, positive-control, and blank groups

phases.phase_name: Day 6: HPLC calibration and fermentation metabolite quantification
dependencies: Day 6: Fermentation sampling and HPLC sample clarification
bottlenecks: Agilent 1260 HPLC booking for an uninterrupted 8 h sequence
Aminex HPX-87H column equilibration and stable 55 °C oven temperature
Calibration curve failure requiring fresh standards or reinjection
Carryover, retention-time drift, or unresolved ethanol/xylose peaks
Autosampler vial errors or insufficient filtrate volume

phases.phase_name: Day 6-7: Data analysis, statistics, interpretation, and results write-up
dependencies: Day 6: HPLC calibration and fermentation metabolite quantification
bottlenecks: Manual integration review for HPLC peaks
Outlier handling with only n=3 biological replicates per group
Assumption checks for ANOVA and Tukey HSD interpretation
Reconciling ethanol yield with xylose consumed if parental control shows background signal

validation.primary_endpoint: Post-thaw viability percentage at immediate and 24h checkpoints.
validation.success_threshold: at least 0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 hours improvement over comparator in replicate-confirmed data.
failure_conditions: No meaningful viability gain in replicate-confirmed runs.
Intervention increases delayed viability loss at 24h.
validation.suggested_statistical_comparison: Two-group comparison against comparator using t-test or Mann-Whitney based on normality.
validation.minimum_replicates_or_design_note: Minimum three biological replicates before execution-level claim.

cro_ready_brief.objective: Determine whether heterologous expression of Piromyces sp. xylose isomerase enables Saccharomyces cerevisiae CEN.PK to convert xylose to ethanol at ≥0.4 g ethanol/g xylose within 48 h under oxygen-limited fermentation.
scope_of_work: Execute pilot-first HeLa cryopreservation comparison between DMSO baseline and trehalose arm.
Deliver replicate-confirmed viability analysis and mitigation traceability report.
cro_ready_brief.sample_count: At least 3 biological replicates per arm
cro_ready_brief.materials_responsibility: CRO to quote and confirm all materials before ordering.
questions_for_cro: Can you support controlled-rate freezing logs in each run?
Can you include replicate-level batch metadata in the final report?
